header.stance: Belly-led Treasury rally meets persistent long-end inflation and supply resistance
header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The June 26 Treasury close extended the week’s duration rally from two through 10 years, yet the long bond’s loss turned the move into a more discriminating verdict on inflation relief. The five-year yield fell three basis points to 4.12%, whereas the 30-year rose one basis point to 4.87%. Cheaper oil, cooler household inflation expectations and a lower New York Fed trend-inflation estimate reduced the need for an imminent policy response. A concentrated technology selloff added demand for intermediate Treasury protection. The curve still refused to endorse an unrestricted duration rally because resilient growth, broad price pressure and a divided Fed kept the terminal policy path uncertain, while fiscal and buyer-base concerns remained concentrated beyond 10 years.

Friday therefore restored the disinflation channel that had driven the June 24 rally after the prior close’s oil rebound briefly reopened the energy tail. Brent fell 4.3449% to $71.99, and both real yield and inflation compensation declined at 10 years. The important change was not a return to a dovish regime. Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari backed one increase this year because inflation had spread beyond energy, and the New York Fed’s growth nowcast remained stable. The close expressed less urgency around timing, especially in the belly, without rejecting the possibility of additional restraint. That combination explains why five-year notes outperformed the front and long ends and why 2s30s steepened even as most nominal yields fell.

Credit and cross assets made the same distinction between relief and confidence. The broad equity index was nearly flat, but semiconductors fell sharply as doubts about AI valuations and capital spending moved from Asian shares into the US session. SpaceX’s $25 billion bond sale then accumulated about $360 million of losses relative to Treasuries by Friday afternoon, showing that exceptional primary capacity did not guarantee stable secondary absorption. Listed credit returns were quiet, so the strain remained concentrated in long-duration technology debt and lower-quality risk. The primary close thesis is consequently a controlled belly rally: energy disinflation and softer expectations lowered immediate policy pressure, while growth resilience, Fed vigilance, technology financing needs and long-end supply risk preserved a high clearing rate for duration and dollar-funded borrowers.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

Treasury performance weakened progressively beyond the belly. The two-year yield fell two basis points to 4.07%, the five-year declined three to 4.12%, and the 10-year eased two to 4.38%. The 30-year moved one basis point higher to 4.87%. Over the week, yields were lower by 12, 11, eight and three basis points at those maturities, respectively. The daily and weekly patterns both place the strongest relief in maturities exposed to the next several policy cycles, while the long bond retained more compensation for inflation persistence, fiscal supply and an uncertain global buyer base.

Curve measures isolated that long-end resistance. The 2s10s spread held at 31 basis points, but 2s30s widened three basis points to 80. The 2s5s10s butterfly fell two basis points to minus 21, confirming five-year outperformance against both neighboring wings. The 2s10s30s butterfly declined three basis points to minus 18 as the 10-year richened against the two-year and 30-year average. This was neither the broad bull flattening of June 24 nor the simple front-end bull steepening that followed the first post-FOMC selloff. It was a rally centered on the policy-growth intersection, with the distant maturity left to carry the unresolved term premium.

Inflation-linked pricing gave the nominal rally an evenly divided composition at 10 years. The 10-year real yield fell one basis point to 2.18%, and the 10-year breakeven also declined one basis point to 2.20%. Five- and seven-year real yields eased one and two basis points to 1.91% and 2.03%, while the 30-year real yield was unchanged at 2.69%. Investors accepted slightly less real compensation in the belly as immediate hike anxiety receded, yet did not relax the real-rate hurdle at the far end. The drop in breakevens showed that cheaper energy was again entering nominal pricing after the previous session’s reversal.

Oil supplied the clearest reason for that change. WTI fell 3.7403% to $69.23, leaving it down 9.6214% over one week, and Brent’s weekly decline approached 10%. Continued tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz reduced the probability that Thursday’s vessel attack would re-establish a lasting physical shortage. The move removed part of the near-term fuel and freight impulse from inflation compensation. It did not settle broader price persistence, because the Fed’s preferred inflation measure had accelerated through May and several officials viewed the pressure as spread across categories.

Household expectations and modeled trend inflation nevertheless reduced the risk of immediate de-anchoring. The University of Michigan survey put five- to 10-year inflation expectations at 3.3%, down from the earlier 3.4% reading. The New York Fed’s Multivariate Core Trend estimate declined to 3.4% in May from a downwardly revised 3.7% in April, with its 68% probability band spanning 3.0% to 3.9%. Services excluding housing remained the largest above-trend contributor, but its excess contribution fell to 0.83 percentage point from 0.91. Goods and housing contributions also moderated, making the improvement broader than a single energy component.

Growth evidence stopped that inflation relief from becoming an easing narrative. The New York Fed Staff Nowcast placed second-quarter real GDP growth at 2.71%, little changed from 2.68% a week earlier. Its wide intervals counsel caution about precision, but the central estimate did not point to an abrupt demand break. Economists surveyed after the Fed meeting also raised their core PCE forecast for the fourth quarter to 3.2% and their job-creation estimates, with the median path keeping the Fed on hold until June 2027. The mixture supports a longer pause more readily than an imminent cut, leaving rates responsive to inflation breadth and labor resilience.

Fed communication reinforced that asymmetry. Kashkari said Friday that widespread inflation led him to include one increase in his projection for this year. Nine of 19 policymakers had penciled in at least one increase after the June meeting. Market pricing still became less aggressive: by Friday, traders assigned roughly a 25% chance to two increases by year-end, down from almost 80% on Monday. The repricing reduced the probability of a rapid sequence, not the possibility of restraint. That is consistent with a lower two-year yield and a richer belly alongside an unchanged long real yield.

The cash-bill curve showed no uniform quarter-end funding disturbance at the close. Four-week coupon-equivalent yield was unchanged at 3.68%, six weeks fell two basis points to 3.67%, and eight weeks declined one to 3.73%. Thirteen weeks fell three basis points to 3.75%, while 17 and 26 weeks eased one basis point to 3.85% and 3.93%. The 52-week yield declined three basis points to 3.93%. Local differences remained, especially against month-earlier increases in the 13- through 52-week area, but Friday’s direction was broadly firmer as the market approached a heavy July 2 settlement date.

Weekend commentary, which came after the New York close, preserved the policy constraint for the next session without explaining Friday’s move. Richmond Fed President Tom Barkin said Sunday that inflation was too high, though falling gasoline and resistance from consumers to price increases offered tentative relief. He also linked some price pressure to AI infrastructure and described a modestly restrictive stance as reasonable. His emphasis on resilient spending and business price-setting echoed the close’s long-end caution: energy can cool quickly, but services, compensation and capital-intensive investment determine whether disinflation becomes durable.

The rates outcome therefore continued the week’s rally while changing its internal message again. June 24 had broadened energy relief into lower real yields, and June 25 had reintroduced breakeven risk when crude rebounded. Friday restored declines in both components and rewarded the five-year sector most. The 30-year selloff, stable growth nowcast and hawkish Fed minority prevented that improvement from becoming a verdict that restrictive policy is finished. The belly carried the best balance of fading shock inflation and future slowdown risk; the long end remained the repository for fiscal, supply and persistence uncertainty.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Broad listed credit returns were subdued. LQD was unchanged at $109.50, while HYG and JNK each declined about 0.06%. Their one-week changes remained modest: LQD gained 0.3942%, HYG lost 0.2250% and JNK fell 0.1764%. The aggregate tape showed neither a generalized financing seizure nor a strong search for lower-quality risk. Treasury gains supported higher-grade duration, while the technology selloff and still-high real-rate structure kept speculative-grade participation cautious.

SpaceX provided direct evidence of post-issuance strain. A dealer quoted the company’s 2056 bonds as much as 28 basis points wider than their issue spread of 175 basis points over Treasuries. The longest maturities erased the tightening achieved during syndication, after demand had once approached $90 billion. Losses on the $25 billion offering grew from roughly $305 million relative to Treasuries late Thursday to about $360 million by Friday afternoon. Five-year notes had attracted the strongest demand, whereas the 20- and 30-year tranches suffered the largest drop-off and subsequent weakness.

The deterioration changed the meaning of the week’s successful placement. Investors had demonstrated capacity to fund a very large investment-grade borrower, yet the secondary market exposed how much of the orderbook may have depended on fast-money demand and an expectation of an immediate trading gain. Years of anticipated negative cash flow, a concentrated governance profile and an upsized transaction made long-dated risk difficult to anchor. Stable SpaceX shares did not repair the bonds because creditors face fixed upside and decades of capital-spending and obsolescence exposure.

Technology credit showed a broader, milder version of that pressure. US high-grade tech risk premiums widened to 0.79 percentage point as of Thursday from 0.74 at the end of May. Alphabet bonds softened after the company announced an $85 billion equity sale, even though new equity ordinarily increases the creditor cushion. The market read the capital raise partly as a signal that AI expenditure and future borrowing could be larger than assumed. Equity issuance improved near-term liquidity while also advertising the scale of the funding cycle.

The supply backdrop explains the sensitivity. US high-grade issuance had reached $175 billion by Tuesday, already a June record. Estimates cited during the weekend placed AI and data-center spending through 2030 at $5.5 trillion and potential high-grade data-center financing over the next five years at $2.1 trillion. Those projections are not completed issuance, but they frame why buyers demanded greater differentiation by maturity and issuer. Capital intensity can keep access open while steadily raising the spread required to absorb another dollar of long-duration technology debt.

Johannesburg demonstrated a different form of creditor prioritization. The city repaid a 1.44 billion rand, or $88 million, bond carrying an 11.455% coupon, completing redemption of its 30 billion rand domestic medium-term-note program. The payment protected bondholders even as the finance minister threatened to withhold 8 billion rand of state funding over a disputed 10.3 billion rand wage agreement. Planned future infrastructure bonds would therefore approach the market after a successful maturity payment but amid severe operating and intergovernmental-finance stress.

The credit close was liquid in aggregate and punitive where duration, leverage or governance made valuation uncertain. Quiet listed-fund returns did not offset SpaceX’s direct spread widening, nor did the initial orderbook settle the borrower’s long-run clearing cost. Technology companies could still raise debt and equity at exceptional scale, and Johannesburg met a contractual payment despite fiscal strain. The common thread was access accompanied by sharper price discovery after funding arrived, with the longest and structurally weakest claims absorbing the greatest adjustment.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

European rates shared the energy relief but retained a policy floor. Traders reduced the amount of additional ECB tightening priced by the end of 2026 to less than a quarter point, after a full increase had been embedded two weeks earlier. The deposit rate stood at 2.25%. German 10-year yields fell to 2.85%, their lowest since March 10, and the euro-area two-year inflation swap moved back below 2.2% from more than 3% in April. Sliding oil allowed duration to recover without signaling that the inflation overshoot had disappeared.

The ECB’s consumer survey clarified the split. One-year inflation expectations fell to 3.5% in May from 4.0%, even before the US-Iran agreement. Three- and five-year expectations remained elevated at 2.9% and 2.4%. Households also expected a 1.7% contraction in output over the next year, less severe than the 2.2% decline anticipated in April, while expected unemployment edged up to 11.3%. Near-term energy fear eased, but longer expectations and weak household growth perceptions left policymakers balancing a still-high inflation path against fragile demand.

Isabel Schnabel’s Saturday presentation arrived after the Treasury close and set a hawkish marker for the coming week. She warned of upside risks across food, goods and services, argued that an energy shock could broaden, and said more rate increases were expected to return inflation to 2%. She also noted no sign of wage pressure and acknowledged that the peace agreement made adverse outcomes less likely. The later remarks did not cause Friday’s Bund or Treasury rally; they underscored why the decline in market-implied tightening remains conditional on national and euro-area inflation releases.

Fiscal information added a separate duration channel. Canada’s first-quarter general-government deficit widened to C$16.4 billion, or 2.1% of GDP, from 1.9% a year earlier. Headline net debt declined to C$538.1 billion and 16.5% of GDP, but debt excluding social-security funds increased 4.3% to C$1.49 trillion. The figures combine a better consolidated balance-sheet ratio with a larger current shortfall and rising debt outside funded social-security assets. Canadian duration therefore faces a less benign supply signal than the headline net-debt decline alone suggests.

France’s state auditor sharpened the longer-term European supply concern in a Saturday account. It said the government lacked a credible spending path to reduce the deficit from 5.1% of output last year to 3% by 2029. Because that assessment arrived after the US close, it belongs to the next global-duration evaluation. Its transmission runs through expected sovereign issuance, French risk premium and the fiscal latitude available before the 2027 presidential election, all of which can keep core and semi-core European term premium elevated even when energy inflation falls.

Asian activity did not support a synchronized global slowdown. Singapore’s May manufacturing output rose 13.0% from a year earlier, led by 35.8% growth in electronics and 32.2% in precision engineering, though total production fell 0.7% on a seasonally adjusted monthly basis. Biomedical output contracted 24.2%, chemicals 11.5% and transport engineering 5.0%. The narrow AI and semiconductor-equipment impulse supports regional demand and capital spending, yet the industry dispersion limits how strongly that growth signal should lift global real yields.

Global duration consequently benefited from a common fall in energy pressure but faced different local constraints. Europe paired lower near-term expectations with still-elevated medium-term gauges and hawkish ECB communication. Canada and France kept fiscal supply in view. Singapore showed strong AI-linked output without broad industrial breadth. These channels help explain the US curve’s maturity split: foreign inflation relief supported the Treasury belly, while fiscal arithmetic, local sovereign alternatives and persistent policy uncertainty maintained the higher clearing hurdle at the long end.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

Emerging-market debt was nearly unchanged at the close. EMB slipped 0.0207% to $96.54, even as the Dollar Index eased 0.0690% to 101.36. The dollar was still 0.5057% higher over one week and 2.2083% over one month, so Friday’s small decline did not remove the external financing constraint created by the Fed’s hawkish shift. Lower oil helped importers and local inflation paths, but countries dependent on foreign inflows remained exposed to US rates and the accumulated strength of the greenback.

Nigeria converted a complex liability structure into immediate cash. The sovereign drew about $1.5 billion from a $5 billion total-return swap with First Abu Dhabi Bank during the preceding weeks. It pledged naira securities equal to 133.3% of the borrowing as collateral. The first tranche carries a spread of 395 basis points over SOFR, rising to 400 basis points thereafter, and the funds are intended to refinance expensive debt and finance the budget deficit. External debt stood at $51.9 billion at the end of December.

The swap improves near-term liquidity while creating a contingent dollar claim. Fitch warned that margin calls payable in dollars could intensify foreign-exchange pressure if domestic yields rise or the naira weakens; the IMF also identified possible constraints on monetary and exchange-rate policy. Similar structures had been used by Angola and Senegal. Nigeria therefore obtained financing outside a conventional bond sale, but the overcollateralization and cross-currency margin mechanism transfer part of the refinancing risk into reserves, local rates and currency management.

The wider EM sensitivity had shifted from oil toward the Fed. The 30-day correlation between US five-year yields and comparable Latin American rates rose to 0.49 from 0.10 at the end of February, with the Mexico relationship above 0.8 in June. Lower crude grants oil importers more inflation room, yet a stronger dollar makes central banks cautious about signaling easing. Turkey and Colombia stand out where reliance on foreign capital can transmit a hawkish US path quickly into local yields and currencies. Friday’s modest EMB move masked that growing macro linkage.

A post-close Chinese release added a two-sided corporate-sovereign backdrop for the next session. Profits at large industrial companies rose 18.8% from a year earlier in January through May and 21.1% in May, supporting taxable income and corporate cash generation. Receivables increased 7.7%, finished-goods inventories 8.8%, and the asset-liability ratio edged up to 58.2%. Stronger earnings reduce immediate credit pressure, but slower cash conversion and higher leverage preserve working-capital demand. EM relief therefore remains country-specific: cheaper energy helps, while dollar funding terms, collateral design and domestic balance-sheet quality determine who can translate it into durable access.

## Cross asset

Equities generated a concentrated safety impulse rather than a broad recession signal. The S&P 500 slipped 0.0472% to 7,354.02, while the Nasdaq 100 fell 1.0940% and the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index dropped 5.2888%. Asian technology weakness had moved through South Korea, where the Kospi lost 5.8%, before reaching US chip shares. Valuation concerns, possible delays to a major AI listing and questions about the returns on hyperscaler spending drove the rotation. The flat broad index showed that the shock was sectoral, but its capital-spending focus linked it directly to growth, inflation and credit supply.

Volatility complicated the haven interpretation. The VIX declined 2.5410% to 18.41 even as semiconductors sold off, and it remained 12.2561% higher over one week. Investors were marking persistent technology dispersion without paying for an escalating system-wide shock at the close. That pattern fits the modest Treasury rally: the belly received some protection demand, while the absence of a volatility surge or broad equity collapse denied the long end a stronger growth-scare bid.

Commodities split between disinflation and hedging. WTI and Brent fell sharply, reinforcing lower breakevens and reducing the imported-cost burden for energy consumers. Gold rose 1.1959% to $4,078.70, though it remained down 3.4422% over one week and 9.3703% over one month. The daily gold gain registered some demand for protection, but the longer decline remained consistent with elevated real yields. Oil’s much larger signal was the renewed retreat in the inflation shock after Thursday’s interruption.

The dollar was nearly unchanged on Friday after a strong monthly advance. Its resilience alongside falling oil means relative policy, not energy scarcity alone, continued to support US currency conditions. Cross assets therefore reinforced the core rates split: concentrated AI stress and cheaper crude favored two- to 10-year Treasuries, while subdued volatility, stable broad equities and an elevated dollar argued against a collapse in nominal demand or funding. SpaceX’s secondary widening supplied the clearest bridge from the equity debate into fixed income, converting doubts about AI returns into a higher long-duration credit premium.

## Forward Market Significance

The US activity and labor sequence from June 29 through July 2 determines whether Friday’s belly rally can survive evidence on demand. The Dallas Fed manufacturing survey arrives Monday at 10:30 a.m. ET, followed Tuesday by JOLTS and consumer confidence, Wednesday by ADP employment and ISM manufacturing, and Thursday at 8:30 a.m. by June payrolls and initial claims. Slower hiring, fewer vacancies and softer manufacturing prices alongside stable output would extend the decline in real yields and confirm that the five-year sector can rally as inflation pressure fades without a sharp recession. Strong payroll and wage growth, resilient vacancies or another rise in factory input prices would lift the two- to five-year policy path, challenge the reduction in expected Fed increases and reopen the post-FOMC tightening impulse.

European inflation and central-bank communication run from Spain’s June flash CPI and the opening of the ECB Forum in Sintra on June 29 through German inflation on June 30 and euro-area flash HICP on July 1. A broad national slowdown followed by softer aggregate headline and core inflation, especially if speakers acknowledge the fall in energy and household expectations, would validate Friday’s reduction to less than a quarter point of additional ECB tightening. Sticky core services, renewed food pressure or guidance resembling Schnabel’s weekend warning would push front-end Bund yields and the euro-area inflation curve higher. That outcome would challenge the idea that the energy reversal alone can lower the global policy floor and could transmit into Treasury term premium through relative rates and foreign demand.

Quarter-end Treasury financing culminates in a July 2 settlement and shortened trading session. Monday brings $92 billion of 13-week and $79 billion of 26-week bills, Tuesday an $80 billion six-week sale, with 17-week supply Wednesday and four- and eight-week auctions Thursday before the recommended 2:00 p.m. ET early close; the market is closed July 3. Broad end-buyer demand, orderly coverage and stable cash-bill yields through settlement would confirm that Friday’s bill rally reflected durable liquidity and would keep funding pressure from contaminating the belly. Dealer-heavy absorption, abrupt cheapening around the settlement turn or a collision with strong payroll data would challenge the orderly-funding interpretation, transmitting first through bills and secured financing before threatening coupon liquidity in a holiday-thinned market.

section_summaries.market_read: The close favors policy-sensitive duration relief without confirming a lower long-run rate regime.
section_summaries.next_check: Durability depends on whether labor strength and broad inflation keep the Fed's restraint bias intact.
section_summaries.summary: Cheaper oil and softer inflation expectations reduced near-term policy urgency, while concentrated technology weakness added protection demand in intermediate Treasuries. Resilient growth, broad inflation pressure, Fed vigilance and long-end fiscal concerns limited the move, leaving a controlled belly-led rally rather than a broad dovish repricing.

section_summaries.market_read: The curve isolates relief in the policy-growth intersection while term premium stays unresolved.
section_summaries.next_check: Payrolls, wages and factory prices must show whether front-end hike risk can keep receding.
section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: Five-year Treasuries led as both real yields and inflation compensation eased, while the long bond retained compensation for persistence, supply and buyer uncertainty. Stable growth and a hawkish Fed minority reduced expectations of rapid tightening without eliminating the risk of further restraint.

section_summaries.market_read: Primary capacity remains ample, but secondary pricing demands more compensation for long risk.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether new technology debt clears cleanly once initial orderbook support fades.
section_summaries.summary: Credit was calm in aggregate but selective after issuance, as SpaceX's long bonds widened despite exceptional placement demand. Large technology financing needs kept market access open while forcing sharper maturity and issuer differentiation, with long-duration and structurally weaker claims bearing the adjustment.

section_summaries.market_read: Foreign energy relief supports the Treasury belly, while fiscal arithmetic props up term premium.
section_summaries.next_check: European inflation breadth and sovereign issuance must confirm that global policy floors are easing.
section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: Global duration benefited from falling energy pressure, but local policy and fiscal constraints prevented a uniform rally. Europe retained an inflation floor, Canada and France kept sovereign supply concerns visible, and narrow Asian manufacturing strength complicated the case for synchronized weakness.

section_summaries.market_read: EM relief remains country-specific because dollar liquidity still dominates local energy benefits.
section_summaries.next_check: Monitor whether weaker currencies or higher domestic yields activate collateral and margin pressure.
section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: EM debt held near flat as cheaper energy offered selective relief but an elevated dollar preserved external financing pressure. Nigeria's collateralized dollar borrowing showed how near-term liquidity can shift refinancing risk into reserves, local yields and currency management when funding terms remain tight.

section_summaries.market_read: Cross assets signal sectoral stress and disinflation relief, not a system-wide demand break.
section_summaries.next_check: The key test is whether technology weakness broadens into volatility and wider funding stress.
section_summaries.summary: Cheaper crude and a concentrated semiconductor selloff supported intermediate Treasury demand, but stable broad equities and softer volatility denied the long end a recession bid. Gold offered limited daily protection, while dollar resilience kept financial conditions restrictive for global borrowers.

section_summaries.market_read: The rally remains conditional on benign macro, inflation and settlement outcomes arriving together.
section_summaries.next_check: Track payroll details, euro-area core inflation and auction absorption through July 2.
section_summaries.summary: Three tests govern whether the belly rally holds: US labor and activity data, European inflation and policy communication, and quarter-end Treasury financing. Softer demand with easing price pressure, broader European disinflation and orderly settlement demand would preserve relief; contrary outcomes would revive policy or funding pressure.

methodology_note: Market levels are shown on an NY-close basis unless otherwise noted.

bullets: 10Y TIPS real yield 2.18% (-1 bp) | 10Y breakeven inflation 2.2% (-1 bp)
UST 2s10s curve 31 bp (-0 bp) | UST 2s30s curve 80 bp (+3 bp)
overall_summary.headline: Cheaper oil lifts the Treasury belly while long-end resistance persists
overall_summary.session_narrative: Treasuries rallied from two through 10 years, with five-year notes leading while the long bond weakened. Falling oil, cooler household expectations and lower modeled trend inflation pulled both real yields and inflation compensation lower, while concentrated technology weakness added protection demand. Stable growth, broad price pressure, divided Fed views and fiscal and buyer-base concerns limited the rally beyond the belly.
overall_summary.summary: The NY close delivered a controlled rally led by intermediate Treasuries as cheaper energy and softer inflation expectations reduced immediate policy pressure. Resilient growth, Fed vigilance and long-duration supply risk kept the move from becoming a broad dovish repricing.

watch_events.market_implication: Strength in payrolls, wages or input prices would lift the two-to-five-year policy path.
watch_events.market_trigger: Slower hiring and softer factory prices with stable output would validate the belly rally.
watch_events.title: US activity and labor sequence
watch_events.watch_line: Track vacancies, hiring, wages, claims and manufacturing prices across the release sequence.

watch_events.market_implication: Sticky services or hawkish guidance would lift Bund front ends and Treasury term premium.
watch_events.market_trigger: Broad inflation slowing plus recognition of lower energy pressure would confirm reduced tightening.
watch_events.title: European inflation and ECB communication
watch_events.watch_line: Compare national and euro-area core inflation with the tone of ECB Forum guidance.

watch_events.market_implication: Dealer-heavy absorption or abrupt cheapening would threaten coupons in thin holiday trading.
watch_events.market_trigger: Orderly coverage and stable bill yields would confirm durable liquidity through settlement.
watch_events.title: Quarter-end Treasury financing
watch_events.watch_line: Watch end-buyer demand, auction coverage and cash-bill behavior into settlement.
